headline: Malaysia's 6% school service tax now in full force at KL's premium international schools
dek: A Malaysian government levy on private education for non-citizen students came into full enforcement at the start of 2026, adding meaningfully to the cost of international schooling in Kuala Lumpur and prompting schools to review how they pass on the charge.
author: marcus-chen
heroAlt: Malaysia's 6% school service tax now in full force at KL's premium international schools

body.text: International schools in Kuala Lumpur serving expatriate families are operating under a new cost regime following Malaysia's extension of its Sales and Service Tax (SST) to cover private education for non-Malaysian students. The 6 per cent levy, which took effect on 1 July 2025 and entered full enforcement — with penalties for non-compliance — from 1 January 2026, applies to any private school charging more than RM 60,000 per year in annual tuition fees per student.

The threshold means the tax falls squarely on Kuala Lumpur's premium international and expatriate schools, where upper-secondary fees at Tier 1 institutions — including Alice Smith, Garden International, IGB International, Marlborough College Malaysia and Epsom College — already run from RM 80,000 to RM 130,000 per year. Schools below the RM 60,000 mark are exempt, and Malaysian citizens are not subject to the charge in any case.

For a family with one child in Year 9 at a Tier 1 school, the realistic all-in annual cost in 2026 is estimated at between RM 110,000 and RM 145,000 (approximately USD 24,000 to USD 32,000), once capital levies, transport, uniform and external examination fees are included alongside the SST. Schools must collect the tax and remit it in full to the government; the International School of Kuala Lumpur, for example, noted in its published fee schedule that it does not retain the SST.

The measure was announced in Malaysia's Budget 2025 and formalised via ministerial order in June 2025. The government stated its aim was to "strengthen the country's fiscal position by increasing revenue and broadening the tax base without adding undue burden on the majority" of citizens. Public education services remain fully exempt.

Observers have noted that while Kuala Lumpur's international school fees remain substantially lower than those in Singapore or Hong Kong — typically 30 to 50 per cent less for an equivalent IB or British curriculum education — the SST narrows that gap for expatriate families, who bear the full cost. The British Council had previously flagged that the expanded tax could affect Malaysia's attractiveness as an education destination, particularly for price-sensitive families from developing countries. Schools and relocation advisers are now recommending that families factor the levy into their budgets from the outset, alongside the range of ancillary costs that are not always visible in headline tuition figures.
